definition: 1. beauty, charm (but see the remarks under halĕp 2.)

sub_entries.definition: beautiful, charming 2. It seems that the meaning ``beautiful'' is not the basic one, but a nuance of it. There is a usage where it does not fit. In our opinion halĕp is more ``the outward form which fits in all respects with a certain state, with what one would expect of ... ''. It comes very close to pantĕs, which is also used by bal.com. as a rendering. ``Fitting'', ``ideal'', ``excellent'' or ``effective'' often give a better translation than ``beautiful''. Halĕpnya may then be more or less ``the form, the appearance of'', in the sense of ``the fitting, ideal form''. Here follow the necessary examples:

citations.text: 132.27: mahalĕp ta sirâtijyotikawaca;
6.9: ahalĕp tĕkap iṅ maṅiriṅ;
1.21: sawaṅ grahagaṇa maṅalih paḍâhalĕp (passim in kak.);
54.31: alĕp aśrī;
2.15a: swaranyâlĕp arum;
3.16b: alĕp dinulu;
etc.

definition: (Skt hastakauśala, skilfulness of hand, manual dexterity). It seems that hastakośala (astakośala) is used in particular for the playing of various musical instruments. As kośala is more or less the same as guṇa, there may be a confusion with aṣṭaguṇa; wīṇārāwaṇahastakośala (AW 31.1) may be wīṇārāwaṇahasta-guṇa. In AW 30.4 it may be: musical composition in general, or a part. melody of that name, as kośala seems also to be used as a name of the Jagaddhita-metre (see KBW s.v. kośala). However, as AW 31 is in that metre, the name kośala may have been taken from this

sub_entries.definition: powder. Is it powder from burnt shells used as collyrium (thus KBW) or more general: powder applied to the face, also to put on (auspicious?) marks (see cĕcĕk)? Women are often said to ``ahawu-hawu'', when sitting outside at the time of evening twilight. Does it have magic significance to ward off the evil influence to which they are particularly exposed at that time?

4b.24: hĕlaṅ anawanakĕn sawuṅ riṅ kuruṅan ṅaranya durācāra maṅiṅgatakĕn strī riṅ dalĕm purī, lwirnya iña, puyaṅ ... The hĕlaṅ weeps because of its longing for rain, just as the kalaṅkyaṅ, q.v., the call of which is also aṅĕlik. Explanation in KBW II 241: ``kĕlik (Jav.), the shrill call of the biḍo, which describes wide circles in the air to ask for rain. Kĕkĕlik, a kind of kite called thus after its cry. It preys on snakes and is speckled; it flies very high and if it comes lower, e.g. to drink at a river, the other birds set on it, so that it longs for rain''. GR s.v. biḍo: ``a bird of prey with grey feathers, larger than the wuluṅ; biḍo kĕlik or manuk kĕlik: a small kind of dappled biḍo''.

definition: It often comes first in the sentence and the grammatical link with the rest of the sentence is often loose so that it comes close to an exclamation (``what a pity'' etc). Could it have arisen from hā iman? This would explain why heman appears as a base-word, although the connection with iman (q.v.) is evident, and the meaning similar to that of meman: what a pity! how unfortunate (that)!; without success, in vain, not worth the effort, of little value, without much force (> easy to answer; Wṛh 47.43).

sub_entries.definition: (avs) to think st. a pity, regret; to have pity on, have consideration with, spare, treat leniently, treat with (undeserved) favour

definition: Problematic; connected with iwaṅ, ilag, (h)ila? In bal. com. hila-hila is now and then rendered with iwag and iwaṅ, iwag (BY 13.33) with iwaṅ. Tentative solution: away (removed, far) from (cf. dūra), far from what is intended, expected or prescribed; improbable, out of the question, absurd, impossible; wrong, inadmissable, against the rules.

sub_entries.definition: 1. prob.: the position (state, attitude) of a hulun (servant, follower) in relation to his lord or master, servitude, submission. Concerning the problems connected with kahulunan, see de Casparis, Prasasti Indonesia I. His derivation from hulu seems unacceptable. 2. In inscr. prob. ``the group of huluns'', and (as a place-name) ``the dwelling-place of huluns''. Occasionally the hulun hyaṅ may be meant in particular. 3. Problematic is śrī kahulunan (once for Kuntī and once for the promulgator of the inscr. of 842). Is it the title of a queen, or perhaps indicating a family relationship: ``aunt'' in a rather general sense, also through marriage or adoption (cf pahulunan)?

definition: god, goddess, deified being, divinity, the part, divinity connected with a place (kraton, wood etc), with flowers, trees, parts of the body, etc (often hyaṅ-hyaṅ); connected with the divine, sacred; person dedicated to the sacred, religious, anchorite, monk, nun. Hyaṅ often before a noun (proper or generic): hyaṅ Indra, saṅ hyaṅ Indra, hyaṅ wulan, saṅ hyaṅ kuṇḍa, ḍaṅ hyaṅ Droṇa, etc). See also kamayaṅan, and under bwat, giri, hulun, palar, tahi, taṅkil.
